BREAKING: Major tech earnings beat expectations
Semiconductors

Global Chip Shortage Eases as New Fabs Come Online

Three new fabrication plants in the US, Germany, and Japan begin production, marking a turning point in the global semiconductor supply chain.

Sarah Kim January 20, 2024 · 6 min read

The global semiconductor shortage that began in 2020 and wreaked havoc on industries from automotive to consumer electronics is finally showing meaningful signs of relief, as three major new fabrication plants begin volume production.

TSMC's Arizona facility, Intel's new fab in Magdeburg, Germany, and Rapidus's pilot plant in Hokkaido, Japan, collectively add an estimated 150,000 wafer starts per month to global capacity.

Impact on the Automotive Industry

"We're seeing lead times drop from 52 weeks back to a more manageable 12-16 weeks for most chip categories," said Dr. Lisa Chen, semiconductor analyst at Gartner. "This is the most significant improvement since the shortage began."

Automakers, who were among the hardest hit by the shortage, have already reported improved supply. Toyota and Volkswagen both raised their 2024 production forecasts in recent weeks.

Geopolitical Implications

The new fabs also represent a shift in the geography of chip manufacturing. For decades, over 90% of advanced chips were made in Taiwan and South Korea. The new US and European plants reduce — but don't eliminate — that concentration.

"Reshoring semiconductor manufacturing is a 10-year project, not a 10-month project," cautioned industry veteran James Park. "But these are real, meaningful steps."

What's Next

Analysts expect the chip market to reach rough supply-demand balance by mid-2024, though shortages may persist in specific categories like advanced automotive sensors and high-bandwidth memory.
